00 The Syllabus

← Back to lessons

You already write JavaScript and TypeScript every day in React and Next.js. You know enough to ship features. This course is about filling the gap between "it works" and "I know exactly why it works."

The rule: mental models first, syntax second. You won't memorize API signatures here. You'll learn the why behind every mechanism so you can derive the how on the fly — the same way a great journalist learns the system before writing the story.

What You'll Be Able to Do

After 12 lessons, you should be able to:

Explain how JS executes, line by line

Call stack, heap, event loop, microtask queue — not buzzwords, but a mental debugger you run in your head.

Read any TypeScript signature cold

Generics, conditional types, mapped types, infer — the advanced type-level programming that library authors use.

Debug without guessing

When something breaks, you'll know which layer caused it: runtime, type system, async scheduling, or scope.

Build production-grade tooling

A type-safe CLI tool with tests, proper error handling, streams, and Node.js internals. Portfolio-ready.

How Lessons Work

Each lesson follows the same structure:

  1. Mental model first. An analogy that makes the concept click before you see a single line of code. Think of it like the lede of a story — it frames everything that follows.
  2. Core concepts. The 2–4 things you need to deeply understand. Explained through comparisons to what you already know (React state, Next.js rendering, journalism workflows).
  3. Code examples. Short, focused blocks with inline explanations. Every block is real, runnable JS or TS.
  4. Quiz. Quick checks: "Can you explain this back?" Click to reveal answers.
  5. Exercise. Something to build or debug. Small enough to finish in one sitting.

Every page also has an AI Tutor in the corner. It knows which lesson you're on, what concepts you've covered, and can explain things differently if you're stuck.

The Mental Models

JavaScript is weird because it evolved in layers — like a city built on top of older cities. These analogies are your map through the layers:

  • The Engine is a newsroom — the call stack is the editor who can only work on one story at a time, the event loop is the assignment desk routing incoming work, and the heap is the filing cabinet where everything is stored.
  • Types are sources — JavaScript loosely categorizes them and sometimes misidentifies them (coercion). TypeScript is the fact-checker that catches errors before publication.
  • Closures are backpacks — a function carries variables from where it was born, even after that birthplace no longer exists. Like a reporter who keeps their source's number long after the beat changes.
  • Prototypes are editorial chains of command — if an object doesn't have a method, it asks its prototype, which asks its prototype, all the way up to the editor-in-chief (Object.prototype).
  • Promises are FOIA requests — you file the request (create the Promise), do other work, and eventually get a response (resolved) or a rejection letter (rejected).
  • Generators are serialized stories — they produce one installment at a time, pausing between each until you ask for the next.
  • TypeScript generics are story templates — the structure is fixed, but the specifics (names, dates, locations) get filled in at write time.

The Roadmap

# Lesson Core Concept
Week 1 — JavaScript Deep Dive
01 Engine Internals How V8 runs your code: call stack, heap, event loop, JIT
02 Types & Coercion 7 primitives, typeof, abstract equality, coercion rules
03 Closures & Scope Lexical scope, closures, hoisting, module pattern
04 Prototypes & Classes Prototype chain, class syntax, inheritance
05 Async Patterns Promises, async/await, microtasks, error handling
Week 2 — TypeScript Mastery
06 Iterators & Generators Symbol.iterator, generators, async iteration
07 TypeScript Foundations Annotations, interfaces, unions, narrowing
08 Generics Generic functions, constraints, conditional types, mapped types
09 Advanced TypeScript Template literals, discriminated unions, branded types
Week 3 — Production Craft
10 Testing Patterns Vitest, mocking, TDD, testing TS code
11 Node.js Internals Event loop (libuv), streams, worker threads
12 Capstone Build a type-safe CLI tool from scratch

Prerequisites

This course assumes you already:

  • Write JavaScript daily (you use React, Next.js, Tailwind)
  • Have used TypeScript annotations (string, number, basic interfaces)
  • Understand async/await at a practical level (but maybe not how it works under the hood)
  • Know what Node.js is and have used npm

If you can build a Next.js page with API routes, you have everything you need. This course takes you from "I can use these tools" to "I understand these tools."

How to Use This Course

One lesson per sitting. Each lesson is designed for a focused 45–90 minute session. Do the reading, try the quiz, then do the exercise. Let it simmer overnight. Understanding compounds — like compound interest, not like cramming for an exam.

The lessons build on each other. Engine internals (01) explains why closures (03) work the way they do. Closures explain why the module pattern works. Prototypes (04) explain why class syntax behaves differently than you'd expect. TypeScript foundations (07) lean on your deep understanding of JS types (02). It's a chain.

That said, if you already feel solid on a topic, skim the lesson and jump to the quiz. If you can answer the quiz questions cold, move on.

The goal isn't completion, it's comprehension. A journalist who understands the system can write any story about it. A developer who understands the runtime can solve any bug in it.

Engine Internals →
